warningLines.explanation: White sacrifices pawns for a huge lead in development and open lines. Black must be very precise here — grabbing too many pawns leads to a devastating attack against the king.

warningLines.name: Advance Variation Bishop Block
warningLines.pgn: e4 c6 d4 d5 e5 Bf5 Nf3 e6 Be2 Nd7 O-O Be7 c3 c5 Nh4 Bg6 Nxg6 hxg6
warningLines.explanation: If Black delays ...c5 too long, White can play Nh4 exchanging off Black's good bishop. Always be ready with ...c5 early to keep the initiative and prevent White from consolidating.

name: Pirc Defence
pgn: e4 d6 d4 Nf6 Nc3 g6 f4 Bg7 Nf3 O-O Bd3 Na6 O-O c5 Be3 Nc7 d5 Bg4 h3 Bxf3 Qxf3 Rb8
overview: The Pirc is a hypermodern defense where you let White build a big pawn center and then systematically undermine it. You fianchetto the dark-squared bishop to g7, castle quickly, and then start chipping away with ...c5 or ...e5. It requires patience and good understanding of when to strike, but the positions are super dynamic and full of counterattacking chances.
keyIdeas: Fianchetto the bishop to g7 to pressure d4 and the long diagonal
Let White overextend in the center, then strike back with ...c5 or ...e5
Castle kingside quickly and play for counterplay on the queenside or center
Use the flexibility of the setup — you can transition into many different structures
traps: After e4 d6 d4 Nf6 Nc3 g6, if White plays f4 and e5 too hastily, ...dxe5 fxe5 Nd5 wins the e-pawn
The ...Qa5 pin on c3 can be annoying for White if the knight is unprotected
warnings: White's Austrian Attack (f4) is very aggressive — you must know the theory or you'll get steamrolled
Don't play too passively or White's space advantage becomes crushing

variations.name: Austrian Attack
variations.pgn: e4 d6 d4 Nf6 Nc3 g6 f4 Bg7 Nf3 O-O Bd3 Na6 O-O c5 d5 Nc7 a4 b6
variations.explanation: The sharpest approach — White plays f4 aiming for a kingside attack. Black must be ready to counterattack in the center with ...c5 and on the queenside. Timing is everything.

variations.name: Classical System
variations.pgn: e4 d6 d4 Nf6 Nc3 g6 Nf3 Bg7 Be2 O-O O-O c6 a4 Nbd7 h3 e5 dxe5 dxe5
variations.explanation: A solid, positional approach from White. Black gets a comfortable game with ...e5 in the center, and the bishop on g7 becomes very active. Good for players who like clear plans.

variations.name: 150 Attack
variations.pgn: e4 d6 d4 Nf6 Nc3 g6 Be3 Bg7 Qd2 O-O f3 c6 Bh6 Bxh6 Qxh6 Qa5 O-O-O Nbd7
variations.explanation: White trades the dark-squared bishops and aims for a kingside attack with h4-h5. Black gets counterplay on the queenside with ...b5, ...Qa5, and ...a5. A critical line to know.

variations.name: Byrne Variation
variations.pgn: e4 d6 d4 Nf6 Nc3 g6 Bg5 Bg7 Qd2 O-O O-O-O c6 f4 b5 Bd3 Qa5
variations.explanation: White develops aggressively with Bg5 and opposite-side castling, leading to mutual attacks. Black strikes on the queenside with ...b5 and ...Qa5 while White goes for the kingside.

variations.name: Lion Variation
variations.pgn: e4 d6 d4 Nf6 Nc3 e5 Nf3 Nbd7 Bc4 Be7 O-O O-O a4 c6 Re1 b5 Bb3 a5
variations.explanation: Black plays ...e5 instead of the fianchetto, creating a Philidor-like structure but with extra flexibility. The ...Nbd7 and ...Be7 setup is very solid.

trapLines.name: Austrian Attack e5 Trick
trapLines.pgn: e4 d6 d4 Nf6 Nc3 g6 f4 Bg7 e5 dxe5 fxe5 Nd5 Nxd5 Qxd5 c3 Qxe5+
trapLines.explanation: If White pushes e5 too early without sufficient preparation, Black captures twice and picks up the e5 pawn with check. White's center collapses and Black has a great position.

trapLines.name: Classical Qa5 Pin
trapLines.pgn: e4 d6 d4 Nf6 Nc3 g6 Nf3 Bg7 Bc4 O-O O-O c6 a4 d5 exd5 cxd5 Bb3 Bg4
trapLines.explanation: Black plays ...d5 at the right moment, opening the center. The bishop goes to g4 pinning the f3 knight, and if White is careless, the d4 pawn falls.

warningLines.name: Austrian Attack Crush
warningLines.pgn: e4 d6 d4 Nf6 Nc3 g6 f4 Bg7 Nf3 O-O e5 Nfd7 e6 fxe6 Ng5 Nf6 Bc4 d5 Nxd5 Nxd5 Bxd5
warningLines.explanation: If you don't react precisely to the e5-e6 push, White's attack crashes through. The e6 sacrifice rips open the position and the bishop on g7 becomes a spectator.

warningLines.name: 150 Attack Kingside Storm
warningLines.pgn: e4 d6 d4 Nf6 Nc3 g6 Be3 Bg7 Qd2 O-O Bh6 Bxh6 Qxh6 e5 d5 Nbd7 O-O-O Nc5 f3 a5
warningLines.explanation: After the dark-squared bishops are traded, Black's king is permanently weakened. If you don't generate queenside counterplay fast with ...a5 and ...b5, White's h4-h5 attack is devastating.

name: Scandinavian Defence
pgn: e4 d5 exd5 Qxd5 Nc3 Qa5 d4 Nf6 Nf3 Bf5 Bc4 e6 Bd2 Bb4 O-O Nc6 a3 Bxc3 Bxc3 Qb6 Re1 O-O Bd3 Bxd3 Qxd3 Rfd8
overview: The Scandinavian is one of the most straightforward and practical responses to 1.e4 — you immediately challenge the center with ...d5 and recapture with the queen. Yes, your queen comes out early, but after ...Qa5 you develop rapidly with ...Nf6, ...Bf5, ...e6, and you get a solid, compact position. It's easy to learn, hard to refute, and leads to positions where understanding trumps memorization.
keyIdeas: Recapture on d5 with the queen and relocate to a5 for active play
Develop the light-squared bishop to f5 BEFORE playing ...e6
Build a solid structure with ...c6, ...e6, ...Nbd7, and castle queenside or kingside
Use the early queen activity to put pressure on White's development
traps: After Qa5, Bd2 Qb6 attacks b2, and if White defends carelessly, Black can win a pawn
The ...Nb4 jump can create tactical threats against c2 and d3 in many lines
warnings: Don't wander with the queen too much — each queen move is a tempo White uses to develop
The ...Qxd5 lines with Nc3 Qa5 d4 require knowing where to put the queen if White plays Bd2

variations.name: Main Line with Qa5
variations.pgn: e4 d5 exd5 Qxd5 Nc3 Qa5 d4 Nf6 Nf3 Bf5 Bc4 e6 Bd2 c6 Qe2 Bb4 O-O-O Nbd7
variations.explanation: The most popular line. Black develops harmoniously with ...Nf6, ...Bf5, ...e6, ...c6. The queen on a5 is well-placed and Black can castle either side depending on White's setup.

variations.name: Nf6 Variation (Modern)
variations.pgn: e4 d5 exd5 Nf6 d4 Nxd5 Nf3 g6 Be2 Bg7 O-O O-O c4 Nb6 Nc3 Nc6 d5 Na5
variations.explanation: Instead of recapturing with the queen, Black plays ...Nf6 and recaptures with the knight. This avoids early queen exposure and leads to a Grunfeld-like structure with the fianchettoed bishop.

variations.name: Icelandic Gambit
variations.pgn: e4 d5 exd5 Nf6 c4 e6 dxe6 Bxe6 d4 Bb4+ Bd2 Qe7 Bxb4 Qxb4+ Qd2 Qxd2+ Nxd2 Nc6
variations.explanation: A sharp gambit line where Black sacrifices a pawn for rapid development and initiative. White gets an extra pawn but Black's pieces are extremely active.

variations.name: Qd6 Variation
variations.pgn: e4 d5 exd5 Qxd5 Nc3 Qd6 d4 Nf6 Nf3 a6 Be2 Bf5 O-O e6 Ne5 Nbd7 Bf4 Qb4
variations.explanation: The queen goes to d6 instead of a5, keeping more central options. This is a flexible choice that avoids some of the theoretical headaches of the Qa5 lines.

variations.name: Portuguese Variation
variations.pgn: e4 d5 exd5 Nf6 d4 Bg4 Be2 Bxe2 Qxe2 Qxd5 Nf3 e6 O-O Nc6 c4 Qd8 Nc3 Be7
variations.explanation: Black develops the bishop to g4 before recapturing the pawn, exchanging it for White's light-squared bishop. This leads to a solid position where Black has no weaknesses.

trapLines.name: Qa5 b2 Trap
trapLines.pgn: e4 d5 exd5 Qxd5 Nc3 Qa5 d4 Nf6 Bd2 Qb6 Nf3 Bg4 Be2 Nc6 O-O O-O-O a3 e5
trapLines.explanation: After ...Qb6 attacking b2, White often plays Nf3 to develop, but Black gets free development with ...Bg4 and ...Nc6. If White isn't careful, the b2 pawn can fall or Black gets a strong attack.

trapLines.name: Nb4 Fork Threat
trapLines.pgn: e4 d5 exd5 Qxd5 Nc3 Qa5 d4 Nf6 Nf3 Bf5 Bc4 e6 O-O Bb4 Bd2 Nc6 a3 Bxc3 Bxc3 Qb6
trapLines.explanation: Black develops rapidly and the pressure on d4 is real. The threat of ...Nb4 hitting c2 forces White to make concessions, and Black gets a comfortable middlegame.

warningLines.name: Queen Wandering Danger
warningLines.pgn: e4 d5 exd5 Qxd5 Nc3 Qe5+ Be2 Bg4 d4 Qa5 Bf3 Bxf3 Qxf3 Nc6 Qd3 e6 Nf3 Bd6
warningLines.explanation: Moving the queen multiple times in the opening wastes precious development time. White gains a lead in development and the initiative. Stick to one queen retreat and focus on piece development.

warningLines.name: c4 Tempo Gain
warningLines.pgn: e4 d5 exd5 Nf6 d4 Nxd5 c4 Nb6 Nc3 e6 Nf3 Bb4 Bd3 O-O O-O Bxc3 bxc3 c5
warningLines.explanation: In the ...Nf6 variation, White's c4 push gains time against the knight and establishes a strong center. Black needs to know this structure well or risk falling into a passive position.

name: Alekhine's Defence
pgn: e4 Nf6 e5 Nd5 d4 d6 Nf3 Bg4 Be2 e6 O-O Be7 c4 Nb6 Nc3 O-O Be3 N8d7 h3 Bh5 Qd2 d5
overview: Alekhine's Defence is a provocative opening where you invite White to push pawns forward and overextend. After 1...Nf6, you're basically saying 'go ahead, grab all that space — I'll show you it's a weakness.' The knight dances around provoking pawn advances, and then you systematically dismantle White's center. It's psychologically challenging for White because their 'advantage' can evaporate quickly.
keyIdeas: Provoke White's pawns forward to create targets for counterattack
Use the knight's retreat to develop pieces with tempo against the extended center
Strike back with ...d6 and ...c5 to undermine the pawn chain
Aim for dynamic piece play once White's center starts to crumble
traps: After e5 Nd5 d4 d6 c4 Nb6 exd6, if White plays carelessly Black recaptures with good development
The pin ...Bg4 against the f3 knight is a recurring tactical theme that pressures d4
warnings: White's Four Pawns Attack is extremely aggressive — precise play is required
Don't let White consolidate a massive center without challenging it

variations.name: Four Pawns Attack
variations.pgn: e4 Nf6 e5 Nd5 d4 d6 c4 Nb6 f4 dxe5 fxe5 Nc6 Be3 Bf5 Nc3 e6 Nf3 Bb4
variations.explanation: White goes all-in with four center pawns. It looks intimidating but Black has excellent counterplay — the extended pawns become targets. ...dxe5 and piece development put enormous pressure on White.

variations.name: Modern Variation
variations.pgn: e4 Nf6 e5 Nd5 d4 d6 Nf3 Bg4 Be2 e6 O-O Be7 c4 Nb6 Nc3 O-O Be3 d5
variations.explanation: The most solid and popular line. Black develops naturally with ...Bg4, ...e6, ...Be7, castles, and then plays ...d5 to establish a firm foothold in the center.

variations.name: Exchange Variation
variations.pgn: e4 Nf6 e5 Nd5 d4 d6 c4 Nb6 exd6 exd6 Nf3 Be7 Be2 O-O O-O Nc6 Nc3 Bf5
variations.explanation: White exchanges on d6 leading to a quieter game. Black gets easy development and a comfortable position. The isolated d6 pawn is not really weak because Black has active pieces.

variations.name: Two Pawns Attack
variations.pgn: e4 Nf6 e5 Nd5 c4 Nb6 d4 d6 exd6 exd6 Nf3 Be7 Be2 O-O O-O Bf5 Nc3 Nc6
variations.explanation: A more restrained version of the Four Pawns Attack. White gets a spatial advantage with c4 and d4, but Black's pieces develop actively and target the d4 pawn.

variations.name: Scandinavian Variation
variations.pgn: e4 Nf6 e5 Nd5 d4 d6 Nf3 dxe5 Nxe5 Nd7 Nxd7 Bxd7 c4 Nb6 Nc3 g6 Be2 Bg7 O-O O-O
variations.explanation: Black transposes into a structure with some Scandinavian flavor. The early exchange on e5 simplifies but Black's position is perfectly healthy with the fianchettoed bishop.

trapLines.name: Four Pawns Attack Counter
trapLines.pgn: e4 Nf6 e5 Nd5 d4 d6 c4 Nb6 f4 dxe5 fxe5 Nc6 Be3 Bf5 Nc3 e6 Nf3 Bb4 Rc1 Bxc3+ bxc3 Nb4
trapLines.explanation: Black's pieces flood into active squares while White's overextended center buckles. The ...Bb4 pin and ...Nb4 hop create tactical threats that are very hard to meet.

trapLines.name: Bg4 Pin Pressure
trapLines.pgn: e4 Nf6 e5 Nd5 d4 d6 Nf3 Bg4 Be2 e6 O-O Be7 c4 Nb6 exd6 cxd6 d5 exd5 cxd5 O-O
trapLines.explanation: After the center opens up, Black's pieces are wonderfully coordinated. The bishop on g4 pins the knight, and Black has pressure along the c-file and against the isolated d5 pawn.

warningLines.name: Four Pawns Steamroll
warningLines.pgn: e4 Nf6 e5 Nd5 d4 d6 c4 Nb6 f4 dxe5 fxe5 g6 Nc3 Bg7 Nf3 O-O Be2 Bg4 O-O Nc6 Be3 f6
warningLines.explanation: If Black plays too passively against the Four Pawns Attack, White's center becomes a steamroller. The key is to challenge it immediately, not let White consolidate and build up an attack.

warningLines.name: Exchange Passive Play
warningLines.pgn: e4 Nf6 e5 Nd5 d4 d6 c4 Nb6 exd6 cxd6 Nc3 g6 Be3 Bg7 Rc1 O-O b3 e5 d5 N8d7 Be2 f5
warningLines.explanation: In the Exchange Variation, if Black doesn't develop actively, the slightly weak d6 pawn becomes a long-term target. Always look for ...e5 or ...Bf5 to activate your pieces.

name: Philidor Defence
pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 d6 d4 Nf6 Nc3 Nbd7 Bc4 Be7 O-O O-O Re1 c6 a4 b6 Be3 Bb7 Qd2 d5
overview: The Philidor Defence is an underrated, incredibly solid choice against 1.e4 e5. Instead of the more common ...Nc6, you play ...d6 which supports the e5 pawn and gives you a very flexible setup. You develop with ...Nf6, ...Be7, ...O-O, and ...Nbd7, creating a compact structure that's tough to crack. The Hanham setup in particular is a fortress that even GMs struggle to break down.
keyIdeas: Support the e5 pawn with ...d6 to create a solid central structure
Use the Hanham setup: ...Nf6, ...Be7, ...O-O, ...Nbd7, ...c6 for maximum solidity
Break with ...d5 when the timing is right to equalize
The knight on d7 can reroute to f8-g6 or b6-c4 depending on the position
traps: After d4 exd4 Nxd4, ...Nf6 e5 looks scary but ...dxe5 is fine
The ...Be7 and ...O-O development avoids many tactical tricks that plague other lines
warnings: Don't play ...exd4 too early or White gets a strong center and open lines for free
The position can become cramped — always have a plan to play ...d5 eventually

variations.name: Hanham Variation
variations.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 d6 d4 Nf6 Nc3 Nbd7 Bc4 Be7 O-O O-O Re1 c6 a4 a5 d5 Nc5
variations.explanation: The main recommended setup. Black builds a super solid position with ...Nf6, ...Nbd7, ...Be7, ...O-O, ...c6. It's nearly impregnable and gives Black long-term chances to play for ...d5.

variations.name: Nimzowitsch Variation
variations.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 d6 d4 Nf6 Nc3 Nbd7 Bc4 Be7 O-O O-O Qe2 c6 Rd1 Qc7 a4 b6 dxe5 dxe5
variations.explanation: A slightly different setup where White plays Qe2 and Rd1 to control the d-file. Black continues with the standard Hanham development and waits for the right moment to break.

variations.name: Exchange Variation
variations.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 d6 d4 exd4 Nxd4 Nf6 Nc3 Be7 Be2 O-O O-O Nbd7 f4 Nc5 Bf3 Re8
variations.explanation: White captures on d4 leading to a more open game. Black develops smoothly and the knight can go to c5 pressuring e4. A good practical choice with clear plans.

variations.name: Antoshin Variation
variations.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 d6 d4 Nf6 dxe5 Nxe4 Bd3 d5 Nbd2 Nxd2 Bxd2 Nd7 O-O Bd6 c4 Nf6
variations.explanation: A dynamic line where after dxe5 Nxe4, Black temporarily wins a pawn. The ensuing complications tend to equalize but Black has active piece play.

variations.name: Lion Variation
variations.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 d6 d4 Nf6 Nc3 Nbd7 Bc4 Be7 O-O O-O a4 c6 Re1 b6 Ba2 Bb7
variations.explanation: The Lion setup adds ...b6 and ...Bb7 to the Hanham formation, giving the light-squared bishop more scope. It's very flexible and can transition into many middlegame structures.

trapLines.name: Legal's Mate Reversal
trapLines.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 d6 Bc4 Bg4 Nc3 Nf6 h3 Bh5 Nxe5 Bxd1 Bxf7+ Ke7 Nd5+ Nxd5 Kxd1 Kf6
trapLines.explanation: White tries the famous Legal's Mate trick with Nxe5, but if you know the defense with ...Kd7 and ...Kc6, Black actually comes out ahead! The king runs to safety and White has sacrificed the queen.

trapLines.name: d4 Capture Refutation
trapLines.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 d6 d4 Nf6 Nc3 Nbd7 Bc4 Be7 Ng5 O-O Bxf7+ Rxf7 Ne6 Qe8 Nxc7 Qd8 Nxa8 b6
trapLines.explanation: White tries a piece sacrifice with Bxf7+ and Ne6, but in the Hanham setup Black is well-prepared. After ...Qe8, Black traps the knight and eventually wins it back with interest.

warningLines.name: Early exd4 Mistake
warningLines.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 d6 d4 exd4 Nxd4 Nf6 Nc3 Be7 Bf4 O-O Qd2 Re8 O-O-O Bf8 f3 a6 g4 b5
warningLines.explanation: Capturing ...exd4 early gives White a strong center and attacking chances. White can castle queenside and launch a kingside pawn storm. This is why the Hanham with ...Nbd7 is preferred.

warningLines.name: Cramped Position Danger
warningLines.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 d6 d4 Nf6 Nc3 Nbd7 Bc4 Be7 O-O O-O Re1 c6 a4 h6 b3 Re8 Bb2 Bf8
warningLines.explanation: If Black plays too passively without a plan for ...d5, the position becomes very cramped. White can slowly build up and Black runs out of useful moves. Always plan the ...d5 break.

name: Petrov Defence
pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nf6 Nxe5 d6 Nf3 Nxe4 d4 d5 Bd3 Nc6 O-O Be7 c4 Nb4 Be2 O-O Nc3 Bf5 a3 Nxc3 bxc3 Nc6 Bf4 Na5
overview: The Petrov (or Russian Game) is the ultimate drawing weapon but don't let that fool you — it's also a very dangerous opening in the right hands. By mirroring White's move with 2...Nf6, you immediately challenge e4 and signal you're not afraid of a fight. The positions are extremely solid for Black, and the theory leads to positions where Black is never worse. World champions like Caruana and Kramnik have used it extensively.
keyIdeas: Mirror White's attack on e5 with ...Nf6 attacking e4
After Nxe5, play ...d6 first (NOT ...Nxe4 immediately) to force the knight back
Build a solid center with ...d5 and develop smoothly
Use the rock-solid structure to play for equality and then slowly outplay your opponent
traps: After Nxe5, if White plays Nxf7?? Qe7 wins the knight — there's no safe square
The ...Nc6 development can lead to tactics against d4 if White isn't careful
warnings: Never play 2...Nxe4 3.Nxe5?? — this is wrong! The correct order is 3...d6 4.Nf3 Nxe4
The Cochrane Gambit (Nxf7) is surprisingly dangerous — study it specifically

variations.name: Classical Variation
variations.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nf6 Nxe5 d6 Nf3 Nxe4 d4 d5 Bd3 Nc6 O-O Be7 c4 Nb4 Be2 O-O Nc3 Bf5
variations.explanation: The main line where both sides develop naturally. Black gets a symmetric pawn structure and active pieces. White has a tiny space advantage but Black's position is completely solid.

variations.name: Steinitz Variation
variations.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nf6 d4 Nxe4 Bd3 d5 Nxe5 Nd7 Nxd7 Bxd7 O-O Bd6 c4 c6 Nc3 Nxc3 bxc3 O-O
variations.explanation: White opens with d4 instead of Nxe5. Black captures on e4 and builds a solid center with ...d5. The resulting positions are very balanced with clear plans for both sides.

variations.name: Three Knights Game
variations.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nf6 Nc3 Nc6 d4 exd4 Nxd4 Bb4 Nxc6 bxc6 Bd3 d5 exd5 cxd5 O-O O-O
variations.explanation: White plays Nc3 instead of Nxe5, leading to a Four Knights-type position. Black develops naturally and the symmetric structure offers equal chances.

variations.name: Cochrane Gambit
variations.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nf6 Nxe5 d6 Nxf7 Kxf7 d4 Be7 Nc3 Re8 Bc4+ Kf8 O-O d5 Be2 c6
variations.explanation: A wild gambit where White sacs the knight on f7! Black should accept and play carefully — ...Be7 and ...Re8 consolidate. The extra piece is usually more important than White's initiative.

variations.name: Italian Variation
variations.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nf6 Nxe5 d6 Nf3 Nxe4 Nc3 Nxc3 dxc3 Be7 Be3 O-O Qd2 Nd7 O-O-O Nf6 Bd3 c5
variations.explanation: White recaptures with the c-pawn and gets an open position with the bishop pair. Black must play actively with ...c5 to prevent White from building a bind.

trapLines.name: Nxf7 Refutation
trapLines.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nf6 Nxe5 Nc6 Nxc6 dxc6 d3 Bc5 Be2 Ng4 O-O Qh4 h3 Nxf2 Rxf2 Bxf2+ Kh1 Qxe4
trapLines.explanation: If White captures the wrong way with Nxc6 allowing ...Bc5 and ...Ng4, Black can launch a devastating kingside attack. The bishop and queen combine for lethal threats.

trapLines.name: Wrong Move Order Punishment
trapLines.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nf6 Nxe5 Nxe4 Qe2 Qe7 Qxe4 d6 d4 dxe5 dxe5 Nc6 Bf4 Qb4+ Qxb4 Nxb4 Na3 Bf5
trapLines.explanation: If Black plays the wrong order with 3...Nxe4, White has Qe2! which is awkward. But after the queen trade, Black has a perfectly playable endgame — know this line to avoid panic.

warningLines.name: Cochrane Gambit Danger
warningLines.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nf6 Nxe5 d6 Nxf7 Kxf7 d4 c5 Nc3 cxd4 Qxd4 Nc6 Qd1 Be7 Bc4+ Be6 Bxe6+ Kxe6
warningLines.explanation: The Cochrane Gambit looks unsound but White gets three pawns and a strong attack for the knight. If you don't know the defensive setup with ...Be7 and ...Re8, White's initiative can be overwhelming.

warningLines.name: d4 Center Bind
warningLines.pgn: e4 e5 Nf3 Nf6 Nxe5 d6 Nf3 Nxe4 d4 d5 Bd3 Bd6 O-O O-O c4 c6 Qc2 Na6 a3 f5 cxd5 cxd5 Nc3 Nxc3 bxc3 Nc7
warningLines.explanation: White's c4 push challenges Black's center immediately. If Black plays too passively, White establishes a bind with c4-c5 or opens the position favorably. Always be ready for the ...c6 defense.

name: Queen's Gambit
pgn: d4 d5 c4 e6 Nc3 Nf6 cxd5 exd5 Bg5 Be7 e3 O-O Bd3 Nbd7 Nf3 Re8 Qc2 c6 O-O Nf8 Bf4 Bg4 Rab1 Nh5 Be5 f6 Bg3 Nxg3 hxg3 Be6
overview: The Queen's Gambit is one of the most principled and classical openings in chess — you immediately fight for central control with 2.c4, putting pressure on Black's d5 pawn. It's not really a gambit since you can always win the pawn back, but the point is to either gain a central pawn majority or saddle Black with an isolated queen's pawn. This opening has been a cornerstone of every world champion's repertoire since Steinitz.
keyIdeas: Pressure d5 with c4 — if Black captures, recapture and enjoy the central majority
Develop pieces to optimal squares: Nc3, Bg5, Nf3, e3, Bd3
In the Exchange Variation, play for the minority attack with a3, b4, b5
Control the center and restrict Black's counterplay
traps: If Black plays ...dxc4, don't panic — play e3 and Bxc4 to recapture with development
In the Exchange, the minority attack b4-b5 creates a weak pawn on c6 that's hard to defend
warnings: Don't push e4 too early without preparation — it can backfire if the center opens prematurely
The QGD gives Black a solid position — don't expect quick wins, play for long-term pressure

variations.name: Exchange Variation
variations.pgn: d4 d5 c4 e6 Nc3 Nf6 cxd5 exd5 Bg5 Be7 e3 O-O Bd3 Nbd7 Qc2 Re8 Nf3 c6 O-O Nf8 Rab1 a5 a3 Ng6 b4 axb4 axb4 Bd6 b5
variations.explanation: White exchanges on d5 early and launches the minority attack with a3, b4, and b5 to undermine Black's queenside pawn chain. The target is the c6 pawn, which becomes backward or isolated after bxc6. White's plan is crystal clear: pile rooks on the b- and c-files and grind down the structural weakness.

variations.name: Classical Mainline
variations.pgn: d4 d5 c4 e6 Nc3 Nf6 Bg5 Be7 e3 O-O Nf3 Nbd7 Rc1 c6 Bd3 dxc4 Bxc4 Nd5 Bxe7 Qxe7 O-O Nxc3 Rxc3 e5 dxe5 Nxe5 Nxe5 Qxe5 Bb3 Bf5 Qd4
variations.explanation: The tabiya of the Queen's Gambit Declined where White develops Bg5, e3, Nf3, Rc1, Bd3 in the classic Capablanca order. After Black releases tension with ...dxc4, White recaptures with the bishop and enjoys a lead in development. The key is that White's pieces coordinate beautifully with Rc1 already on the open file, and the initiative persists well into the middlegame.

variations.name: Tartakower Variation
variations.pgn: d4 d5 c4 e6 Nc3 Nf6 Bg5 Be7 e3 O-O Nf3 h6 Bh4 b6 cxd5 Nxd5 Bxe7 Qxe7 Nxd5 exd5 Rc1 Be6 Qa4 c5 Qa3 Rc8 Bb5 Nd7 dxc5
variations.explanation: Black plays ...h6 and ...b6 to fianchetto the light-squared bishop, a favorite of Tartakower. White should exchange on d5 to fix the pawn structure, then target the hanging pawns or isolated d-pawn. The Qa4-a3 maneuver pressures both the queenside and the c5 break. White's edge lies in the superior pawn structure and piece activity along the c-file.

variations.name: Queen's Gambit Accepted
variations.pgn: d4 d5 c4 dxc4 Nf3 Nf6 e3 e6 Bxc4 c5 O-O a6 Qe2 b5 Bb3 Bb7 Rd1 Nbd7 Nc3 Qb8 d5 exd5 Nxd5 Nxd5 Bxd5 Bxd5 Rxd5 Be7
variations.explanation: Black grabs the c4 pawn immediately. White recovers it effortlessly with e3 and Bxc4, gaining a tempo on development. The central pawn majority (d4 + e3 vs Black's e6) gives White a lasting space advantage. The d5 break is the thematic lever that opens the position in White's favor, especially with a rook already on d1.

variations.name: Slav Defence Response
variations.pgn: d4 d5 c4 c6 Nf3 Nf6 Nc3 dxc4 a4 Bf5 e3 e6 Bxc4 Bb4 O-O O-O Qe2 Nbd7 e4 Bg6 Bd3 Bxd3 Qxd3 Re8 Bg5 Qa5 e5 Nd5 Bd2
variations.explanation: Against the Slav with ...c6, White plays a4 immediately to prevent Black from consolidating with ...b5. The e4 push seizes the center and gains space. After e5, the knight on f6 is pushed away and White gets a strong central bind. The a4 move is critical: without it, Black holds the extra pawn comfortably.

variations.name: Semi-Slav Response
variations.pgn: d4 d5 c4 c6 Nf3 Nf6 Nc3 e6 e3 Nbd7 Bd3 dxc4 Bxc4 b5 Bd3 Bb7 O-O a6 e4 c5 d5 e5 a4 b4 Nb1 c4 Bc2 Bc5 Nbd2
variations.explanation: The Semi-Slav with ...c6 and ...e6 is one of the richest structures in chess. White aims for the e4 push to claim the center, and if Black allows d5, the passed pawn becomes a monster. The sharp tactical play after e4 c5 d5 leads to complex middlegames where White's central space advantage and piece activity provide lasting initiative.

variations.name: Catalan Transposition
variations.pgn: d4 d5 c4 e6 Nf3 Nf6 g3 Be7 Bg2 O-O O-O dxc4 Qc2 a6 Qxc4 b5 Qc2 Bb7 Bd2 Nd5 Nc3 Nxc3 Bxc3 Nd7 e4 Rc8 Rfe1
variations.explanation: White fianchettoes with g3 and Bg2 instead of the classical e3/Bd3 setup, transposing into a Catalan structure. The bishop on g2 is an absolute monster on the long diagonal, pressuring b7 and c6. Combined with Qc2, White creates a battery that makes it very hard for Black to develop the queenside comfortably. This hybrid approach combines QGD piece placement with Catalan diagonal power.

variations.name: Anti-QGD: Early Bf4 System
variations.pgn: d4 d5 c4 e6 Nc3 Nf6 Bf4 Bd6 Bg3 O-O e3 c5 dxc5 Bxc5 Nf3 Nc6 cxd5 exd5 Be2 d4 exd4 Nxd4 O-O Be6 Nxd4 Bxd4 Bg4 Re8
variations.explanation: Instead of the standard Bg5, White develops the bishop to f4 early, avoiding the main theoretical lines of the QGD. This sidesteps the Tartakower and Lasker variations entirely. If Black challenges with ...Bd6, the retreat to g3 maintains the bishop and after c5 dxc5, White enjoys a comfortable IQP position with active pieces and clear attacking prospects against Black's king.

trapLines.name: QGA e4 Thrust
trapLines.pgn: d4 d5 c4 dxc4 e4 e5 Nf3 exd4 Bxc4 Nc6 O-O Be6 Bxe6 fxe6 Qb3 Qd7 Qxb7 Rb8 Qa6 Nf6 Nxd4 Nxd4 Bg5 Be7 Bxf6 Bxf6 Nc3
trapLines.explanation: After Black takes ...dxc4, White seizes the center with the aggressive e4 thrust. If Black matches with ...e5, the position opens and White's superior development tells quickly. The Qxb7 raid wins a pawn while maintaining pressure, and after Nxd4 the centralized pieces dominate. Black's shattered pawn structure on e6 is a permanent liability.

trapLines.name: Minority Attack Breakthrough
trapLines.pgn: d4 d5 c4 e6 Nc3 Nf6 cxd5 exd5 Bg5 Be7 e3 O-O Bd3 Nbd7 Nf3 Re8 Qc2 c6 O-O Nf8 Rab1 a5 a3 Ng6 b4 axb4 axb4 Bd6 b5 cxb5 Rxb5 Bc7 Rb3
trapLines.explanation: The minority attack in full execution: White plays a3, b4, b5 targeting the c6 pawn. When bxc5 arrives, Black is left with either a backward c-pawn on an open file or an isolated d-pawn, both chronic weaknesses. White's rook swings to b3 to triple on the b-file. This plan has won countless grandmaster games because Black has no active counterplay against it.

trapLines.name: Exchange Variation Knight Outpost
trapLines.pgn: d4 d5 c4 e6 Nc3 Nf6 cxd5 exd5 Bg5 Be7 e3 O-O Bd3 c6 Qc2 Nbd7 Nf3 Re8 O-O Nf8 Ne5 Ng6 f4 Ne4 Bxe7 Qxe7 Nxe4 dxe4 Bc4
trapLines.explanation: In the Exchange Variation, White can establish a powerful knight on e5 supported by f4. This outpost is nearly impossible to dislodge and controls critical squares d7, c6, and f7. If Black tries to challenge with ...Nf8-g6, White reinforces with f4 and the knight becomes a permanent fixture dominating the position.

warningLines.name: Premature e4 Push
warningLines.pgn: d4 d5 c4 e6 Nc3 Nf6 Bg5 Be7 e4 dxe4 Nxe4 Nxe4 Bxe7 Qxe7 Qd2 Nc6 O-O-O e5 d5 Nd4 Nf3 Nxf3 gxf3 O-O Qe3 Bf5
warningLines.explanation: Pushing e4 without proper preparation lets Black equalize easily. After the simplifying sequence ...dxe4 Nxe4 Nxe4 Bxe7, pieces fly off the board and Black gets active play with ...e5 and ...Nd4 with tempo. White's shattered kingside pawns after gxf3 are a lasting weakness. Always prepare e4 with Nf3 and Bd3 first.

warningLines.name: Slav b5 Consolidation
warningLines.pgn: d4 d5 c4 c6 Nf3 Nf6 Nc3 dxc4 e3 b5 a4 b4 Na2 e6 Bxc4 Be7 O-O O-O Qe2 Bb7 Nc1 a5 Nd3 Nd5 Rd1 Nb6 Bb3 N8d7
warningLines.explanation: If White plays e3 instead of a4 against the Slav, Black can consolidate with ...b5 and hold the extra pawn. The knight retreat to a2 is extremely awkward, wasting two tempi. White struggles to regain the pawn and Black develops comfortably. Always play a4 early against the Slav to prevent this consolidation.

warningLines.name: Semi-Slav Meran Counterattack
warningLines.pgn: d4 d5 c4 c6 Nf3 Nf6 Nc3 e6 e3 Nbd7 Bd3 dxc4 Bxc4 b5 Bd3 a6 e4 c5 e5 cxd4 Nxb5 axb5 exf6 gxf6 O-O Bb7 Qe2 Bd6
warningLines.explanation: The Meran Variation of the Semi-Slav gives Black powerful counterplay with ...b5 and ...c5 challenging White's center. After e5 cxd4 Nxb5, the position is razor-sharp and a single inaccuracy from White can be fatal. Black's bishop pair and open g-file provide dangerous attacking chances against White's king.

name: London System
pgn: d4 Nf6 Nf3 g6 Bf4 Bg7 e3 d6 Be2 O-O O-O Nbd7 h3 b6 c3 Bb7 Nbd2 Ne4 Nxe4 Bxe4 Qc2 Bb7
overview: The London System is the ultimate low-maintenance opening — you develop Bf4, e3, Nf3, Bd3, Nbd2, c3 in almost any order regardless of what Black does. It's incredibly reliable and gives you a solid, playable position against virtually anything. The dark-squared bishop on f4 is the star piece, and from there you can launch kingside attacks or play a positional game depending on what Black gives you.
keyIdeas: Develop Bf4 early — this is the signature move, get it out before e3 blocks it in
Build a solid pyramid with d4, e3, c3 — hard to break down
Place pieces on their best squares: Nf3, Bd3, Nbd2, O-O, then choose your plan
If Black plays ...Nh5 to trade the bishop, retreat Bg3 to maintain the pair
traps: If Black plays ...Qb6 hitting b2, don't panic — Qb3 or Rb1 handles it easily
After Bg3, if Black plays ...Nh5, the bishop retreats and the knight on h5 is misplaced
warnings: Don't play Bf4 if Black has already played ...Bf5 — the trade favors Black in many lines
Against the King's Indian setup (...g6, ...Bg7), be ready for ...c5 and ...Qb6 pressure

variations.name: London vs Queen's Pawn
variations.pgn: d4 d5 Bf4 Nf6 e3 e6 Nf3 c5 c3 Nc6 Nbd2 Bd6 Bg3 O-O Bd3 Qe7 O-O e5
variations.explanation: The standard London against ...d5. White builds a solid center and Black aims for ...e5 to free the position. After ...e5, White can play dxe5 or maintain the tension.

variations.name: London vs King's Indian
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 Bf4 g6 e3 Bg7 Nf3 O-O Be2 d6 O-O Nbd7 h3 b6 c3 Bb7 Nbd2 e5
variations.explanation: Against the King's Indian setup, White develops with Be2 instead of Bd3 (since the f5 square isn't available to Black's bishop). Solid and reliable with long-term plans.

variations.name: Jobava London
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 Bf4 d5 Nc3 e6 e3 c5 Nf3 Nc6 Be2 Bd6 Bg3 O-O O-O b6 Ne5 Bb7
variations.explanation: A more aggressive version where White plays Nc3 instead of c3. This allows for more dynamic play with ideas of e4 and central expansion. Growing in popularity at all levels.

variations.name: London vs Grunfeld Setup
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 Bf4 g6 e3 Bg7 Nf3 d5 Be2 O-O O-O c5 c3 Nc6 Nbd2 Bf5 Nh4 Be6
variations.explanation: Against ...d5 and ...g6, White proceeds normally. The key is to complete development before deciding on a plan. The Nh4 idea can be used to redeploy the knight to f5.

variations.name: London vs Dutch
variations.pgn: d4 f5 Bf4 Nf6 e3 e6 Nf3 d5 Bd3 Bd6 Bxd6 Qxd6 O-O O-O c4 c6 Nc3 Ne4
variations.explanation: Against the Dutch (...f5), the London is a great choice. White develops naturally and the bishop trade on d6 gives White a nice structural advantage. The c4 push challenges Black's center.

trapLines.name: Nh5 Misplacement
trapLines.pgn: d4 d5 Bf4 Nf6 e3 e6 Nf3 Bd6 Bg3 Nh5 Bd3 Nxg3 hxg3 Nd7 c3 Qe7 Nbd2 e5 dxe5 Nxe5 Nxe5 Bxe5 Qh5 g6 Qh4
trapLines.explanation: Black trades the bishop with ...Nh5, but White gets the h-file and attacking chances. The half-open h-file combined with Qh5 ideas gives White a dangerous kingside initiative.

trapLines.name: c5 Qb6 Handling
trapLines.pgn: d4 d5 Bf4 Nf6 e3 c5 c3 Qb6 Qb3 c4 Qc2 Bf5 Qc1 e6 Nf3 Nbd7 Nbd2 Be7 Be2 O-O O-O Rac8
trapLines.explanation: Black tries to exploit the b2 weakness with ...Qb6, but after Qb3 exchanging queens or Qc2, Black's queen is just displaced. White continues with normal development and a solid position.

warningLines.name: Early Bf5 Counter
warningLines.pgn: d4 d5 Bf4 Bf5 e3 e6 Nf3 Nf6 c4 Bxb1 Rxb1 Bb4+ Ke2 dxc4 a3 Be7 Nd2 O-O
warningLines.explanation: If Black plays ...Bf5 early, trading bishops with Bxb1 gives Black an easy game. White should consider c4 quickly or adjust the move order to avoid this simplification.

warningLines.name: King's Indian Counterattack
warningLines.pgn: d4 Nf6 Bf4 g6 Nf3 Bg7 e3 d6 Be2 O-O O-O Nbd7 h3 Qe8 c3 e5 Bh2 Nh5 dxe5 dxe5
warningLines.explanation: Against the King's Indian, if White isn't careful, ...e5 followed by ...Nh5 gives Black active piece play. The dark-squared bishop retreats and Black can take over the center.

name: Catalan Opening
pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 g3 d5 Bg2 Be7 Nf3 O-O O-O dxc4 Qc2 a6 Qxc4 b5 Qc2 Bb7 Bd2 Nc6 Nc3 Nb4 Qb1 Be4
overview: The Catalan is one of the most sophisticated and powerful openings in modern chess — it combines the Queen's Gambit pawn structure with a fianchettoed bishop on g2. That bishop is an absolute monster on the long diagonal, exerting pressure from g2 all the way to a8. The positions look quiet but are deceptively dangerous for Black. Nearly every top player in the world has this in their repertoire, and for good reason.
keyIdeas: The fianchettoed bishop on g2 creates long-term pressure on the queenside
If Black takes ...dxc4, White often plays Qc2 and recovers the pawn with positional compensation
White has a persistent space advantage and better piece coordination
The bishop on g2 combines with Qc2 to create powerful pressure on c6 and the long diagonal
traps: After ...dxc4, the bishop on g2 rakes down the long diagonal hitting the rook on a8
If Black tries to hold the c4 pawn with ...b5, a4 undermines it and wins the pawn back with interest
warnings: Don't rush to recapture on c4 — the positional pressure is often more valuable than the pawn
Against the Closed Catalan (...c6 without ...dxc4), be patient and avoid premature pawn breaks

variations.name: Open Catalan
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 g3 d5 Bg2 dxc4 Nf3 Be7 O-O O-O Qc2 a6 Qxc4 b5 Qc2 Bb7 Bd2 Be4 Qc1 Bb7
variations.explanation: Black takes on c4 and White gets the powerful bishop on g2 plus easy piece development. White recovers the pawn naturally and maintains long-term positional pressure.

variations.name: Closed Catalan
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 g3 d5 Bg2 Be7 Nf3 O-O O-O Nbd7 Qc2 c6 Nbd2 b6 e4 Bb7 e5 Ne8
variations.explanation: Black keeps the center closed with ...c6. White plays for e4 to seize space. The position is strategic and slow-burning, but White's bishop on g2 and space advantage provide long-term pressure.

variations.name: Open Catalan with a6-b5
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 g3 d5 Bg2 dxc4 Nf3 a6 O-O b5 Ne5 Nd5 a4 Bb7 axb5 axb5 Rxa8 Bxa8
variations.explanation: Black tries to hold the pawn with ...a6 and ...b5, but White's a4 break undermines the queenside pawns. The resulting positions favor White due to the dominant bishop on g2.

variations.name: Catalan with Nbd7
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 g3 d5 Bg2 Be7 Nf3 O-O O-O dxc4 Qc2 a6 a4 Bd7 Qxc4 Bc6 Bf4 Nbd7 Nc3 Nb6 Qd3 Nbd5
variations.explanation: Black develops with ...Nbd7 and aims for ...c5 to free the position. White plays a4 to clamp down on the queenside and maintains the bishop's pressure on the diagonal.

variations.name: Catalan vs Slav Setup
variations.pgn: d4 d5 c4 c6 Nf3 Nf6 g3 dxc4 Bg2 b5 Ne5 Bb7 a4 a6 axb5 cxb5 b3 cxb3 Qxb3 e6
variations.explanation: Against the Slav setup with ...c6, the Catalan bishop still shines. White undermines Black's queenside pawns with a4 and b3, while the bishop on g2 dominates the long diagonal.

trapLines.name: Long Diagonal Pressure
trapLines.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 g3 d5 Bg2 dxc4 Nf3 Nc6 O-O Rb8 Nc3 a6 e4 b5 d5 exd5 exd5 Na5 d6 Bb7 Re1+ Be7 dxe7 Qxe7 Rxe7+ Kxe7
trapLines.explanation: White's bishop on g2 creates devastating pressure down the long diagonal. Combined with the d5 push and e4 advance, the center opens and Black's position collapses under the diagonal pressure.

trapLines.name: a4 Pawn Break
trapLines.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 g3 d5 Bg2 dxc4 Nf3 a6 O-O b5 a4 Bb7 axb5 axb5 Rxa8 Bxa8 Ne5 Nd5 Nc3 Nxc3 bxc3 Bd5 Nxc4 Bxg2 Kxg2 bxc4 Qa4+ Nd7 Qxc4
trapLines.explanation: The a4 break is the signature tactic. It destroys Black's queenside pawn structure, and combined with Ne5 targeting c4 and c6, White wins back the pawn with a dominant position.

warningLines.name: Closed Catalan Patience
warningLines.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 g3 d5 Bg2 Be7 Nf3 O-O O-O Nbd7 Qc2 c6 Nbd2 b6 e4 dxe4 Nxe4 Nxe4 Qxe4 Bb7 Qe2 c5 Rd1 Rc8
warningLines.explanation: The Closed Catalan requires patience. If White pushes e4 too early, Black can equalize with ...dxe4 and ...c5. The bishop on g2 is less effective in a closed center — wait for the right moment.

warningLines.name: b5 Hold Attempt
warningLines.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 g3 d5 Bg2 dxc4 Nf3 b5 a4 c6 O-O Bb7 axb5 cxb5 Ne5 a5 b3 cxb3 Qxb3 Bd5 Qb2 Bd6
warningLines.explanation: If White doesn't challenge ...b5 accurately, Black can consolidate the extra pawn and get a solid position. The a4 break must be timed precisely or Black holds the pawn structure together.

name: Trompowsky Attack
pgn: d4 Nf6 Bg5 Ne4 Bf4 d5 e3 c5 Bd3 Nd6 Nf3 Nc6 O-O Nf5 c3 e6 Nbd2 Be7 Qc2 O-O e4 dxe4 Nxe4 Bd7
overview: The Trompowsky Attack is a brilliant way to sidestep all of Black's prepared Indian defenses. By playing 2.Bg5 immediately, you force Black to deal with the pin on the f6 knight before they can set up their favorite system. It's a practical weapon that gets opponents out of their comfort zone from move two — and the positions are surprisingly rich.
keyIdeas: 2.Bg5 forces Black to react immediately, preventing standard Indian setups
After 2...Ne4 3.Bf4, White gets the bishop pair and a space advantage
The pawn structure often resembles a reversed London with more attacking chances
White can play for e3-e4 or c4 breaks depending on Black's response
traps: After 2...Ne4 3.Bh4? g5! 4.Bg3 Nxg3 5.hxg3, Black has won the bishop pair and damaged White's structure — so play 3.Bf4! instead.
If Black plays 2...d5 3.Bxf6 exf6, White gets a pleasant position with e3 and Bd3, while Black's doubled f-pawns are a permanent weakness.
warnings: Don't play Bg5 and then retreat to h4 on ...Ne4 — always go to f4 to keep the bishop active
Against 2...e6, be ready for a French-type structure — don't overextend in the center

trapLines.name: Ne4 Bh4 Blunder
trapLines.pgn: d4 Nf6 Bg5 Ne4 Bh4 g5 Bg3 Nxg3 hxg3 d5 Nf3 Bg7 e3 c5 Bd3 Nc6 Nc3 cxd4 exd4 Bg4
trapLines.explanation: Retreating to h4 instead of f4 allows Black to win the bishop pair with ...g5 and ...Nxg3, leaving White with doubled g-pawns and no compensation.

trapLines.name: Vaganian Gambit Trick
trapLines.pgn: d4 Nf6 Bg5 Ne4 Bf4 c5 d5 Qb6 Bc1 e6 dxe6 fxe6 Nc3 Nxc3 bxc3 d5 e3 Bd6 Nf3 O-O Bd3 Nd7
trapLines.explanation: In the Vaganian Gambit after 4...c5 5.d5 Qb6, White retreats Bc1 and gambits the d-pawn. After dxe6 fxe6, White has a lead in development and Black's e6 pawn is weak.

variations.name: 2...Ne4 Main Line
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 Bg5 Ne4 Bf4 d5 e3 c5 Bd3 Nc6 Nf3 Nf6 O-O Bg4 c3 e6 Nbd2 Bd6 Bxd6 Qxd6
variations.explanation: The most common continuation. Black plays ...Ne4 to challenge the bishop, and after 3.Bf4, White gets a comfortable game with the bishop pair. The position is solid and White can maneuver for a kingside attack or queenside expansion.

variations.name: 2...d5 Bxf6 Line
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 Bg5 d5 Bxf6 exf6 e3 Bd6 Bd3 O-O Nd2 Re8 Ne2 c5 c3 Nc6 O-O cxd4 exd4
variations.explanation: Taking on f6 gives Black doubled pawns but the bishop pair. White plays a solid setup with e3, Bd3, and short castle. The doubled f-pawns are a long-term weakness that White can exploit in the endgame.

variations.name: 2...e6 French Structure
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 Bg5 e6 e4 h6 Bxf6 Qxf6 Nc3 d6 Qd2 Nd7 O-O-O Be7 f4 O-O Nf3 c5 Bc4 a6
variations.explanation: Against 2...e6, White can transpose into an aggressive system with e4. The position resembles a French Defense where White has already developed the dark-squared bishop actively. The h6/Bxf6 exchange gives White a strong center.

variations.name: Raptor Variation (2...c5)
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 Bg5 c5 d5 Qb6 Nc3 Qxb2 Bd2 Qb6 e4 d6 f4 e6 Bc4 exd5 exd5
variations.explanation: The Raptor Variation arises after 2...c5 3.d5 Qb6. White gambits the b2 pawn for rapid development and a huge center. The position is sharp and White gets excellent attacking chances after Bd2, e4, and f4.

variations.name: 2...g6 Fianchetto Response
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 Bg5 g6 Bxf6 exf6 c4 d5 cxd5 Qxd5 Nc3 Qa5 e3 Bg7 Bd3 O-O Nge2 Re8 O-O Nd7
variations.explanation: When Black fianchettoes, White takes on f6 and plays for a structural advantage. The doubled f-pawns give Black the bishop pair but White has the better pawn structure and can play for a gradual positional squeeze.

warningLines.name: Overextending with e4 Too Early
warningLines.pgn: d4 Nf6 Bg5 Ne4 Bf4 d5 e3 Nc6 Nd2 Nxd2 Qxd2 e6 Ngf3 Bd6 Bxd6 Qxd6 c3 O-O Bd3 e5 dxe5 Qxe5
warningLines.explanation: Pushing e4 too early in the Ne4 Bf4 line gives Black counterplay. After ...dxe4, White's center is gone and Black can develop smoothly. Stick with the solid e3 approach.

warningLines.name: Ignoring c5 Break
warningLines.pgn: d4 Nf6 Bg5 Ne4 Bf4 d5 e3 c5 c3 Nc6 Nd2 Nxd2 Qxd2 cxd4 exd4 Bf5 Bd3 Bxd3 Qxd3 e6 Nf3 Bd6 Bxd6 Qxd6
warningLines.explanation: If White plays too passively without challenging Black's c5 break, Black equalizes easily. White needs to develop quickly with Bd3 and Nf3 to maintain the initiative.

name: Queen's Gambit Declined
pgn: d4 d5 c4 e6 Nc3 Nf6 Bg5 Be7 e3 O-O Nf3 Nbd7 Rc1 c6 Bd3 dxc4 Bxc4 Nd5 Bxe7 Qxe7 O-O Nxc3 Rxc3 e5 Bb3 exd4 exd4 Nf6 Re1 Bg4
overview: The Queen's Gambit Declined is one of the most solid and respected openings in chess. By playing ...e6, you support your d5 pawn and prepare to develop naturally. The positions are strategic and rich — you're playing the same lines that Capablanca and Karpov used to win World Championships. It's all about understanding pawn structures and piece placement.
keyIdeas: Support d5 with ...e6, then develop pieces to natural squares
The ...dxc4 break at the right moment frees Black's position
After ...Nd5, Black gets active piece play and challenges the Bg5
The minority attack with ...b5-b4 on the queenside is a key strategic plan
traps: Elephant Trap: After Bg5 Nbd7, if White plays cxd5 Nxd5 Bxe7 Qxe7, Black can spring ...Nxc3! winning the queen if White recaptures bxc3 because of ...Qa3+.
After dxc4 Bxc4, Black plays ...Nd5 hitting the Bg5 and gaining tempo to reorganize.
warnings: Don't play ...dxc4 too early — wait until you can follow up with ...c5 or ...Nd5
Be careful about the e6 pawn becoming a weakness in the endgame — keep the position dynamic

trapLines.name: Elephant Trap
trapLines.pgn: d4 d5 c4 e6 Nc3 Nf6 Bg5 Nbd7 cxd5 exd5 Nxd5 Nxd5 Bxd8 Bb4+ Qd2 Bxd2+ Kxd2 Kxd8
trapLines.explanation: The famous Elephant Trap springs after White carelessly takes on d5 twice. Black recaptures with the knight, and when White greedily plays Bxd8, Bb4+ forks the king and queen. After the simplifications Black reaches a level endgame, but the trap wins material against unprepared opponents. Always know this pattern when playing the QGD with ...Nbd7.

trapLines.name: Lasker Trap with Central Break
trapLines.pgn: d4 d5 c4 e6 Nc3 Nf6 Bg5 Be7 e3 O-O Nf3 Nbd7 Rc1 c6 Bd3 dxc4 Bxc4 Nd5 Bxe7 Qxe7 O-O Nxc3 Rxc3 e5 dxe5 Nxe5 Nxe5 Qxe5
trapLines.explanation: After the key ...Nd5, Black challenges White's dark-squared bishop and gains tempo. The follow-up ...e5 break blows open the center while Black's pieces are perfectly coordinated. White's rook on c3 is awkwardly placed after the exchanges, and Black achieves full equality with active piece play. This central break is the thematic idea behind the entire Orthodox Defense.

trapLines.name: Cambridge Springs Queen Trap
trapLines.pgn: d4 d5 c4 e6 Nc3 Nf6 Bg5 Nbd7 Nf3 c6 e3 Qa5 Nd2 Bb4 Qc2 O-O Be2 e5 O-O exd4 exd4 dxc4 Bxf6 Nxf6
trapLines.explanation: The Cambridge Springs sets a cunning trap with ...Qa5 pinning the Nc3 along the a5-e1 diagonal. If White plays carelessly, Black's queen and bishop create deadly threats against the pinned knight. The ...Bb4 follow-up intensifies the pressure, and White must be very precise to avoid losing material. This is one of the most practical traps in tournament play.

variations.name: Orthodox Defense Main Line
variations.pgn: d4 d5 c4 e6 Nc3 Nf6 Bg5 Be7 e3 O-O Nf3 Nbd7 Rc1 c6 Bd3 dxc4 Bxc4 Nd5 Bxe7 Qxe7 O-O Nxc3 Rxc3 e5 dxe5 Nxe5 Nxe5 Qxe5 Bb3 Bg4 Qd4 Qe6
variations.explanation: The most classical continuation of the QGD, played by Capablanca and Karpov. Black develops solidly with Be7, castles, and plays Nbd7 before timing the ...dxc4 break perfectly. After ...Nd5 challenges the bishop and ...e5 opens the center, Black achieves full equality with active pieces. The key is understanding that ...dxc4 and ...Nd5 must come together as a package — the knight on d5 is the cornerstone of Black's counterplay.

variations.name: Tartakower Variation
variations.pgn: d4 d5 c4 e6 Nc3 Nf6 Bg5 Be7 e3 O-O Nf3 h6 Bh4 b6 cxd5 Nxd5 Bxe7 Qxe7 Nxd5 exd5 Rc1 Be6 Qa4 c5 Qa3 Rc8 Bb5 a6 dxc5 bxc5 Be2 Qb7
variations.explanation: Karpov's beloved system where Black plays ...h6 and ...b6, developing the light-squared bishop actively. After the exchange on d5, Black gets the bishop pair and pressure along the c-file and long diagonal. The ...Bb7 plan gives Black excellent control of the light squares, and the ...c5 break challenges White's center. This variation is extremely solid and has been used successfully at the highest level for over a century.

variations.name: Lasker Defense
variations.pgn: d4 d5 c4 e6 Nc3 Nf6 Bg5 Be7 e3 O-O Nf3 h6 Bh4 Ne4 Bxe7 Qxe7 cxd5 Nxc3 bxc3 exd5 Qb3 Qd6 c4 dxc4 Bxc4 Nd7 O-O Nb6 Bd3 Bd7 Rfc1 Rfc8
variations.explanation: Lasker's brilliant simplifying idea plays ...Ne4 early, forcing exchanges that relieve Black's cramped position. After ...Nxc3 and bxc3, White has doubled c-pawns that become a long-term weakness. Black follows up with ...c5 to challenge the center and targets the isolated c-pawns. This is the ultimate equalizing weapon in the QGD — simple to play, hard to crack, and it has neutralized many a strong White player.

variations.name: Exchange Variation
variations.pgn: d4 d5 c4 e6 Nc3 Nf6 cxd5 exd5 Bg5 Be7 e3 O-O Bd3 Nbd7 Nf3 Re8 Qc2 c6 O-O Nf8 Rab1 Ng6 b4 a6 a4 Bd6 b5 Bc7 bxc6 bxc6
variations.explanation: White exchanges on d5 early to create a symmetrical pawn structure, then launches the minority attack with b4-b5. Black must defend accurately on the queenside while seeking kingside counterplay. The key defensive idea is ...Nf8-g6, rerouting the knight to support both flanks. Despite the symmetry, these positions are rich with strategic nuance — Black should aim for piece activity on the kingside with ...Ng6, ...Bd6, and potentially ...Nf4.

variations.name: Ragozin Variation
variations.pgn: d4 d5 c4 e6 Nc3 Nf6 Nf3 Bb4 Bg5 dxc4 e4 c5 Bxc4 cxd4 Nxd4 Qa5 Bd2 Qc5 Nb3 Qe7 Qf3 O-O O-O-O a5 a3 Bxc3 Bxc3 a4 Nd4 Nbd7
variations.explanation: The Ragozin blends ideas from the Nimzo-Indian and QGD — Black plays ...Bb4 pinning the knight and creating immediate tension. After ...dxc4 and ...c5, Black gets dynamic piece play and challenges White's center directly. The resulting positions are sharper than the classical QGD, with Black often getting counterplay against White's king if it castles queenside. This variation rewards players who like active, concrete play over slow maneuvering.

variations.name: Vienna Variation
variations.pgn: d4 d5 c4 e6 Nc3 Nf6 Nf3 dxc4 e3 a6 Bxc4 b5 Bd3 Bb7 O-O Nbd7 e4 c5 d5 e5 b3 Be7 Nh4 O-O Nf5 Bc8 Nxe7+ Qxe7 Bg5 h6 Bh4
variations.explanation: The Vienna Variation features Black's early ...dxc4 capture, conceding the center temporarily but gaining time to develop the queenside with ...a6, ...b5, and ...Bb7. White gets a strong center but Black's queenside expansion creates counterplay. The resulting positions are complex and double-edged, with Black aiming for the ...c5 break to undermine White's center. This is a practical choice for players who prefer concrete play to theoretical battles.

variations.name: Cambridge Springs
variations.pgn: d4 d5 c4 e6 Nc3 Nf6 Bg5 Nbd7 Nf3 c6 e3 Qa5 Nd2 Bb4 Qc2 O-O Bd3 dxc4 Bxc4 e5 O-O exd4 exd4 Nb6 Bd3 Bg4 f3 Bh5 Nde4 Nxe4 Nxe4
variations.explanation: The Cambridge Springs with ...Qa5 is a sharp counterattacking system where Black exploits the pin on the Nc3 along the a5-e1 diagonal. After ...Bb4 intensifies the pin, White faces difficult decisions about how to untangle. Black gets active piece play and targets White's center, with the queen on a5 coordinating beautifully with the bishop on b4 and knight on d7. This variation has been a reliable weapon for over a century and remains dangerous at all levels.

variations.name: Bf4 QGD
variations.pgn: d4 d5 c4 e6 Nc3 Nf6 Bf4 Be7 e3 O-O Nf3 Nbd7 c5 Nh5 Bd3 Nxf4 exf4 b6 b4 a5 a3 Ba6 Bxa6 Rxa6 O-O g6 Qe2 axb4 axb4 bxc5 bxc5
variations.explanation: When White avoids Bg5 entirely and plays Bf4, the character of the game changes significantly. Black doesn't face the pin on the Nf6, so the light-squared bishop problem is less acute. Black can aim for ...Nh5 to challenge the Bf4 directly, or develop with ...b6 and ...Ba6 exchanging light-squared bishops. The resulting positions are quieter but strategically rich, with play revolving around the queenside pawn structure and the fight for control of e5.

warningLines.name: Passive Setup Without ...c5 or ...dxc4
warningLines.pgn: d4 d5 c4 e6 Nc3 Nf6 Bg5 Be7 e3 O-O Nf3 Nbd7 Bd3 b6 O-O Bb7 cxd5 exd5 Ne5 Nxe5 dxe5 Ne8 Bf4 c5 Qg4 g6 f4 Nc7 Rf3 f5 exf6 Bxf6 Rh3
warningLines.explanation: Playing too passively without the timely ...dxc4 or ...c5 break allows White to build a dominating position. The bishop on b7 bites into the granite d5 pawn, and White can launch a dangerous kingside attack. The e5-pawn becomes a permanent thorn in Black's position, and the lack of counterplay means Black is defending for the entire game. Always ensure you have an active plan before committing to a pawn structure.

warningLines.name: Early ...dxc4 Without Follow-up
warningLines.pgn: d4 d5 c4 e6 Nc3 Nf6 Bg5 dxc4 e3 b5 a4 c6 axb5 cxb5 Nxb5 Qa5+ Nc3 Bb4 Bxc4 O-O Nf3 Ba6 Bxa6 Nxa6 Qb3 Nc7 O-O Bxc3 bxc3 Nce8 Nd2
warningLines.explanation: Taking on c4 too early without the standard preparation of ...Be7, ...O-O, and ...Nbd7 gives White easy play. The attempt to hold the pawn with ...b5 creates queenside weaknesses that White exploits with a4. Black ends up with weak pawns and undeveloped pieces, while White's centralized pieces dominate. The correct approach is to develop first and capture on c4 only when ...Nd5 is ready.

warningLines.name: Allowing White's Kingside Attack in Exchange Variation
warningLines.pgn: d4 d5 c4 e6 Nc3 Nf6 cxd5 exd5 Bg5 Be7 e3 O-O Bd3 Nbd7 Qc2 Re8 Nge2 Nf8 O-O-O c6 Kb1 Ng6 h4 Be6 Ng3 Qa5 h5 Nf8 Nf5 Bxf5 Bxf5 Bd8
warningLines.explanation: In the Exchange Variation, if Black plays too passively and allows White to castle queenside and launch a kingside pawn storm, the position becomes very dangerous. White's h4-h5 advance combined with pieces aimed at the kingside creates serious threats. Black must seek active counterplay with ...b5 and the minority attack on the queenside, or find the right moment for ...c5 to open the center before White's attack becomes overwhelming.

name: Queen's Gambit Accepted
pgn: d4 d5 c4 dxc4 Nf3 Nf6 e3 e6 Bxc4 c5 O-O a6 Qe2 b5 Bb3 Bb7 Rd1 Nbd7 Nc3 Be7 e4 O-O Bg5 cxd4
overview: The Queen's Gambit Accepted is a pragmatic and dynamic choice. You take the pawn on c4 not to hold it, but to develop freely while White recaptures. After ...e6 and ...c5, Black gets an active position with easy piece development. It's been a favorite of players like Anand who love active piece play over static structures.
keyIdeas: Take on c4 to free your position, then develop with ...e6, ...c5, and ...Nf6
The ...b5 push gains queenside space and prepares ...Bb7 on the long diagonal
After ...c5, Black challenges White's center directly and gets dynamic play
Don't try to hold the c4 pawn — develop pieces instead
traps: After e3 e6 Bxc4 c5, if White plays d5 too early, Black gets ...exd5 Bxd5 Nxd5 Qxd5 with a great position.
If White delays recapturing on c4, Black can play ...b5 and ...Bb7 to keep the pawn with a good game.
warnings: Don't try to hold the c4 pawn with ...b5 too early — it weakens your queenside
Be careful about the a6 pawn becoming a target after ...a6 and ...b5

trapLines.name: Premature d5 Refutation
trapLines.pgn: d4 d5 c4 dxc4 Nf3 Nf6 e3 e6 Bxc4 c5 d5 exd5 Bxd5 Nxd5 Qxd5 Be6 Qd1 Nc6 O-O Be7 Nc3 O-O
trapLines.explanation: If White pushes d5 prematurely, Black recaptures cleanly and after the queen exchange, Black's lead in development and active bishops give an excellent position.

trapLines.name: b5 Counter-attack
trapLines.pgn: d4 d5 c4 dxc4 Nf3 Nf6 e3 e6 Bxc4 c5 O-O a6 a4 Nc6 Qe2 cxd4 Rd1 Be7 exd4 O-O Nc3 Nb4
trapLines.explanation: When White plays a4 to prevent ...b5, Black switches plans with ...Nc6 and ...Nb4, targeting the d3 square. The knight on b4 is a powerful outpost that disrupts White's coordination.

variations.name: Classical Main Line
variations.pgn: d4 d5 c4 dxc4 Nf3 Nf6 e3 e6 Bxc4 c5 O-O a6 Qe2 b5 Bb3 Bb7 Rd1 Nbd7 Nc3 Qb6
variations.explanation: The main line of the QGA. Black develops naturally with ...e6, ...c5, and ...a6/b5, getting a solid position with the powerful bishop on b7. White has a small space advantage but Black's pieces are very active.

variations.name: Smyslov Variation (3...a6)
variations.pgn: d4 d5 c4 dxc4 Nf3 a6 e3 Nf6 Bxc4 e6 O-O c5 Qe2 Nc6 Rd1 b5 Bb3 cxd4 exd4 Be7
variations.explanation: Smyslov's idea of 3...a6 immediately prepares ...b5 to hold the c4 pawn or at least gain queenside space. It's a flexible move that keeps Black's options open for various pawn structures.

variations.name: Sadler Variation (3...Nf6 4.e3 Bg4)
variations.pgn: d4 d5 c4 dxc4 Nf3 Nf6 e3 Bg4 Bxc4 e6 h3 Bh5 Nc3 Nbd7 g4 Bg6 Nh4 Be4 Nxe4 Nxe4 f3 Nd6 Bb3
variations.explanation: The Bg4 line is an active approach where Black develops the bishop outside the pawn chain before playing ...e6. It leads to sharp positions where both sides have chances.

variations.name: Central Variation (3.e4)
variations.pgn: d4 d5 c4 dxc4 e4 e5 Nf3 exd4 Bxc4 Nc6 O-O Nf6 Ng5 d3 Nxf7 Qe7 Nxh8 Bg4 Qd2 O-O-O
variations.explanation: Against the aggressive 3.e4, Black plays ...e5 striking back in the center. White gets an open game but Black's pieces develop quickly. The line is sharp but fundamentally sound for Black.

variations.name: Alekhine Variation (4.Nc3)
variations.pgn: d4 d5 c4 dxc4 Nf3 Nf6 Nc3 a6 e4 b5 e5 Nd5 a4 Nxc3 bxc3 Qd5 g3 Bb7 Bg2 Qd8
variations.explanation: White plays Nc3 before e3, going for the e4 push. Black counters with ...a6 and ...b5, holding the c4 pawn and building queenside play. The resulting positions are dynamic with chances for both sides.

warningLines.name: Holding c4 Too Long
warningLines.pgn: d4 d5 c4 dxc4 Nf3 b5 a4 c6 axb5 cxb5 b3 cxb3 Qxb3 a6 Nc3 Nf6 e4 e6 Be2 Bb4 Bd2 O-O
warningLines.explanation: Trying to hold the extra c4 pawn with ...b5 leads to a weakened queenside. White opens lines with a4 and b3, getting excellent piece activity against Black's exposed pawns.

warningLines.name: Missing the ...c5 Break
warningLines.pgn: d4 d5 c4 dxc4 Nf3 Nf6 e3 e6 Bxc4 Be7 O-O O-O Nc3 a6 Qe2 b6 Rd1 Bb7 e4 Nbd7 e5 Nd5 Nxd5 exd5 Bd3 c6
warningLines.explanation: Without the timely ...c5 break, Black gets a passive position. White advances e4-e5 and dominates the center. Always look for the ...c5 break to challenge White's center.

name: Slav Defence
pgn: d4 d5 c4 c6 Nf3 Nf6 Nc3 dxc4 a4 Bf5 e3 e6 Bxc4 Bb4 O-O Nbd7 Qe2 Bg6 e4 O-O Bg5 h6 Bh4 Re8
overview: The Slav Defence is the thinking player's response to the Queen's Gambit. By supporting d5 with ...c6 instead of ...e6, you keep the light-squared bishop free to develop outside the pawn chain. This is crucial — in the QGD, that bishop often gets stuck behind the e6 pawn. The Slav gives you solid structure AND active pieces.
keyIdeas: ...c6 supports d5 while keeping the light-squared bishop's diagonal open
The bishop goes to f5 or g4 before Black plays ...e6
After ...dxc4, Black develops rapidly with ...Bf5, ...e6, and ...Bb4
The pawn structure is super solid — c6 and d5 form a strong wall
traps: After Nc3 dxc4 a4 Bf5, if White plays e3 and Bxc4, Black has already solved the light-squared bishop problem that plagues the QGD.
In the Exchange Slav, if White plays Bf4 too early, Black can play ...Qb6! hitting b2 and creating threats.
warnings: Don't play ...dxc4 too early without ...c6 support — the structure needs preparation
Be careful about ...Bf5 timing — in some lines White plays Qb3 attacking b7 and f7 simultaneously

trapLines.name: Qb3 Trap Defense
trapLines.pgn: d4 d5 c4 c6 Nf3 Nf6 Nc3 dxc4 a4 Bf5 Qb3 Na6 e4 Bg6 Bxc4 Nb4 Be2 e6 O-O Be7 Rd1 O-O
trapLines.explanation: White plays Qb3 hitting b7 and f7 simultaneously. Black defends calmly with ...Na6! The knight heads to b4 and the queen's threats are neutralized. This is a critical pattern to know.

trapLines.name: Exchange Slav Qb6 Trick
trapLines.pgn: d4 d5 c4 c6 cxd5 cxd5 Bf4 Nc6 e3 Qb6 Qb3 Nf6 Nc3 Bf5 Nf3 e6 Be2 Bb4 O-O O-O
trapLines.explanation: In the Exchange Slav, after Bf4 Black plays ...Nc6 and ...Qb6! pressuring b2. White must be careful, and if they respond poorly, Black gets easy equality with active piece play.

variations.name: Main Line (a4 Bf5)
variations.pgn: d4 d5 c4 c6 Nf3 Nf6 Nc3 dxc4 a4 Bf5 e3 e6 Bxc4 Bb4 O-O Nbd7 Qe2 Bg6
variations.explanation: The main tabiya of the Slav. Black develops the bishop to f5 before playing ...e6, solving the light-squared bishop problem. After ...Bb4 and ...Nbd7, Black has harmonious development and a rock-solid structure.

variations.name: Exchange Variation
variations.pgn: d4 d5 c4 c6 cxd5 cxd5 Bf4 Nc6 e3 Nf6 Bd3 Bg4 Nf3 e6 Nbd2 Bd6 Bxd6 Qxd6 O-O O-O
variations.explanation: White exchanges early for a quiet, strategic game. Black develops naturally and the minority attack with ...b5-b4 is often the key plan. Don't underestimate these positions — they're deceptively complex.

variations.name: Chebanenko Variation (4...a6)
variations.pgn: d4 d5 c4 c6 Nf3 Nf6 Nc3 a6 e3 Bf5 Be2 e6 O-O Nbd7 c5 Be7 b4 O-O Bb2 Ne4
variations.explanation: The Chebanenko with 4...a6 is a modern approach. Black prepares ...b5 and keeps options flexible. The a6 pawn prevents Nb5 and supports the queenside expansion. It's become very popular at the top level.

variations.name: Quiet Variation (4.e3)
variations.pgn: d4 d5 c4 c6 Nf3 Nf6 e3 Bf5 Nc3 e6 Nh4 Bg6 Nxg6 hxg6 Bd3 Nbd7 O-O Bd6 c5 Bc7
variations.explanation: In the quiet 4.e3 line, Black develops the bishop to f5 immediately. After Nh4 Bg6 Nxg6 hxg6, Black gets an open h-file for attacking chances. The doubled g-pawns are actually a strength in the middlegame.

variations.name: Geller Gambit
variations.pgn: d4 d5 c4 c6 Nf3 Nf6 Nc3 dxc4 e4 b5 e5 Nd5 a4 e6 axb5 Nxc3 bxc3 cxb5 Ng5 Bb7
variations.explanation: White gambits a pawn with e4 and e5, chasing Black's knight and seizing space. Black must be precise — after ...Nd5 and ...e6, the position is sharp but defensible. The extra pawn gives Black long-term chances.

warningLines.name: Premature ...Bf5 Before ...c6
warningLines.pgn: d4 d5 c4 c6 Nf3 Bf5 cxd5 cxd5 Qb3 Qb6 Nc3 Qxb3 axb3 Nc6 Bf4 e6 e3 Bb4 Ne5 Nf6 Be2 O-O O-O a6
warningLines.explanation: Playing ...Bf5 before completing development can be premature. White plays Qb3 hitting b7 and d5, and after the queen exchange, White has a pleasant endgame advantage with better pawn structure.

warningLines.name: Ignoring White's e4 Push
warningLines.pgn: d4 d5 c4 c6 Nf3 Nf6 Nc3 dxc4 a4 Bf5 e3 e6 Bxc4 Bb4 O-O O-O Qe2 Bg6 e4 e5 d5 Nbd7 Nh4 Nc5 Nxg6 hxg6 f3 a5
warningLines.explanation: If Black ignores White's e4 push and plays passively, White gets a commanding center. The e4-e5 advance cramps Black's position. Always be ready to challenge e4 with ...e5 at the right moment.

name: Semi-Slav Defence
pgn: d4 d5 c4 c6 Nf3 Nf6 Nc3 e6 Bg5 h6 Bh4 dxc4 e4 g5 Bg3 b5 Be2 Bb7 O-O Nbd7 Ne5 Bg7 Nxd7 Nxd7 Bh5 O-O
overview: The Semi-Slav is one of the deepest and most challenging openings in chess — it's been tested at the very highest level for decades. You combine the solid Slav structure (...c6) with the flexible ...e6, and the resulting positions are incredibly rich. The Botvinnik Variation alone has more theory than most entire openings. If you love deep, complex positions where preparation matters, this is your weapon.
keyIdeas: The ...c6 and ...e6 setup is ultra-solid but keeps both center tension and piece activity
In the Meran, ...dxc4 and ...b5 leads to a sharp queenside expansion
In the Botvinnik, ...dxc4 e4 g5 grabs material but invites a fierce White attack
The light-squared bishop development is the key strategic problem to solve
traps: Botvinnik Variation: After e4 g5 Bg3 b5, Black grabs two pawns and dares White to attack — but the theory is extremely deep and one wrong move is fatal.
In the Anti-Meran with Bd3, Black can play ...dxc4 Bxc4 b5 Bd3 a6 followed by ...c5 with a great game.
warnings: The Botvinnik Variation requires extensive memorization — don't play it unprepared
Be careful about the e6 pawn becoming weak after ...dxc4 if you don't follow up correctly

trapLines.name: Botvinnik Material Grab
trapLines.pgn: d4 d5 c4 c6 Nf3 Nf6 Nc3 e6 Bg5 dxc4 e4 b5 e5 h6 Bh4 g5 Nxg5 hxg5 Bxg5 Nbd7 g3 Bb7 Bg2 Qb6 exf6 O-O-O O-O c5
trapLines.explanation: The Botvinnik Variation is one of the sharpest lines in chess. Black grabs two pawns but their king walks a tightrope. After ...O-O-O, Black has excellent chances if they know the theory — the extra material counts.

trapLines.name: Anti-Meran c5 Break
trapLines.pgn: d4 d5 c4 c6 Nf3 Nf6 Nc3 e6 e3 Nbd7 Bd3 dxc4 Bxc4 b5 Bd3 a6 e4 c5 d5 e5 O-O Bb7
trapLines.explanation: In the Anti-Meran, Black plays ...dxc4 Bxc4 b5 and then ...c5, challenging White's center. The bishop on b7 becomes very powerful once the long diagonal opens. This is the standard equalizing plan.

variations.name: Meran Variation
variations.pgn: d4 d5 c4 c6 Nf3 Nf6 Nc3 e6 e3 Nbd7 Bd3 dxc4 Bxc4 b5 Bd3 Bb7 O-O a6 e4 c5 d5 e5
variations.explanation: The Meran is one of the great theoretical battlegrounds. Black plays ...dxc4 and ...b5, gaining queenside space while White pushes for e4. The resulting positions are sharp and double-edged, requiring precise play from both sides.

variations.name: Botvinnik Variation
variations.pgn: d4 d5 c4 c6 Nf3 Nf6 Nc3 e6 Bg5 dxc4 e4 b5 e5 h6 Bh4 g5 Nxg5 hxg5 Bxg5 Nbd7
variations.explanation: The Botvinnik is the ultimate test of memorization and nerves. Black grabs two pawns and says 'come and get me.' The resulting positions are among the most analyzed in chess history. Not for the faint of heart.

variations.name: Anti-Meran (Bd3 without Bg5)
variations.pgn: d4 d5 c4 c6 Nf3 Nf6 Nc3 e6 e3 Nbd7 Bd3 dxc4 Bxc4 b5 Bd3 a6 e4 c5 e5 cxd4 Nxb5 axb5 exf6 gxf6
variations.explanation: The Anti-Meran leads to a different type of game. White avoids Bg5 and plays for a direct e4 push. Black responds with the typical ...b5 and ...c5 breaks. The positions are less forcing than the Meran but equally rich.

variations.name: Stoltz Variation
variations.pgn: d4 d5 c4 c6 Nf3 Nf6 Nc3 e6 e3 Nbd7 Qc2 Bd6 Bd3 O-O O-O dxc4 Bxc4 b5 Bd3 Bb7
variations.explanation: The Stoltz Variation with Qc2 is a quieter approach. White develops the queen early to control e4 and eyes the kingside. Black responds with standard development and the ...b5/...c5 plan.

variations.name: Moscow Variation (Bg5 h6 Bxf6)
variations.pgn: d4 d5 c4 c6 Nf3 Nf6 Nc3 e6 Bg5 h6 Bxf6 Qxf6 e3 Nd7 Bd3 dxc4 Bxc4 g6 O-O Bg7
variations.explanation: White takes on f6 immediately, damaging Black's structure but giving up the bishop pair. Black recaptures with the queen and fianchettoes the dark-squared bishop. The position is solid and Black has long-term chances with the two bishops.

warningLines.name: Botvinnik Without Preparation
warningLines.pgn: d4 d5 c4 c6 Nf3 Nf6 Nc3 e6 Bg5 dxc4 e4 b5 e5 h6 Bh4 g5 Nxg5 hxg5 Bxg5 Nbd7 g3 Bb7 Bg2 Qb6 exf6 O-O-O O-O b4 Na4 Qb5 a3 Nc5 axb4 Nxa4 Rxa4 Qxb4
warningLines.explanation: The Botvinnik requires extremely precise play. One wrong move and Black's extra pawns mean nothing against White's attack. If you don't know the theory deeply, choose the Meran or Moscow instead.

warningLines.name: Passive Play After e3
warningLines.pgn: d4 d5 c4 c6 Nf3 Nf6 Nc3 e6 e3 Nbd7 Bd3 Be7 O-O O-O Qc2 b6 e4 dxe4 Nxe4 Nxe4 Bxe4 Bb7 Rd1 Qc7 Bd3 c5 dxc5 Nxc5 Be2 Rfd8
warningLines.explanation: Playing ...Be7 and ...O-O without the ...dxc4/...b5 plan gives Black a passive position. White gets to play e4 in comfort and Black's pieces lack active prospects. Always strive for the ...b5 expansion.

name: King's Indian Defence
pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 g6 Nc3 Bg7 e4 d6 Nf3 O-O Be2 e5 O-O Nc6 d5 Ne7 Ne1 Nd7 Be3 f5 f3 f4 Bf2 g5 Nd3 Nf6 c5 Ng6 Rc1 Bd7
overview: The King's Indian Defence is the ultimate fighting weapon for Black. You let White build a big center with d4, c4, and e4, then you blow it apart with ...e5 and ...f5. The positions are incredibly dynamic — Black often launches a kingside attack while White pushes on the queenside. Kasparov used this to win some of his most brilliant games. It's not for positional purists — it's for warriors.
keyIdeas: Let White build the center, then strike with ...e5 and ...f5
After d5 Ne7, Black regroups for the f5 pawn storm on the kingside
The Bg7 is Black's strongest piece — it controls the long diagonal a1-h8
The typical plan is ...f5, ...f4, ...g5, ...Nf6-h5-f4 with a devastating kingside attack
traps: In the Classical Main Line, after f3 f5 exf5 gxf5, Black gets a huge attack on the g-file if White plays passively.
If White tries to close the queenside too early with c5, Black can sacrifice with ...f4 and ...g5 breaking through on the kingside.
warnings: Don't play ...e5 if White hasn't played e4 — you might transpose into other openings
Be careful about the d6 pawn becoming weak if your attack doesn't work out

trapLines.name: Kingside Pawn Storm
trapLines.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 g6 Nc3 Bg7 e4 d6 Nf3 O-O Be2 e5 O-O Nc6 d5 Ne7 Ne1 Nd7 f3 f5 g4 Kh8 Ng2 Nf6 Be3 fxg4 fxg4 Nh5
trapLines.explanation: When White tries to stop ...f5 with g4, Black plays ...Kh8 and ...fxg4, opening the f-file. The knight heads to h5 then f4, and Black's attack on the kingside becomes overwhelming.

trapLines.name: Central Exchange Tactic
trapLines.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 g6 Nc3 Bg7 e4 d6 Nf3 O-O Be2 e5 dxe5 dxe5 Qxd8 Rxd8 Nxe5 Nxe4 Nxe4 Bxe5 Bf3 Nc6 Be3 Bf5 Nd2 Nd4
trapLines.explanation: If White captures on e5 in the exchange variation, Black gets active piece play. The Bg7 becomes a monster and Black's pieces coordinate beautifully. The endgame is easy to play for Black.

variations.name: Classical Main Line (Mar del Plata)
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 g6 Nc3 Bg7 e4 d6 Nf3 O-O Be2 e5 O-O Nc6 d5 Ne7 Ne1 Nd7 f3 f5
variations.explanation: The Mar del Plata variation is the heavyweight championship of the King's Indian. Both sides launch all-out attacks on opposite wings. Black pushes ...f5-f4-g5-g4 while White plays c5-c6 on the queenside. It's a race to see who breaks through first.

variations.name: Saemisch Variation
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 g6 Nc3 Bg7 e4 d6 f3 O-O Be3 e5 d5 Nh5 Qd2 f5 O-O-O Nd7
variations.explanation: The Saemisch is White's most aggressive setup with f3 and Be3, often followed by Qd2 and O-O-O. Black responds with the standard ...e5 and ...f5, but the game gets even sharper with opposite-side castling. Both sides attack at full speed.

variations.name: Four Pawns Attack
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 g6 Nc3 Bg7 e4 d6 f4 O-O Nf3 c5 d5 e6 Be2 exd5 cxd5 Re8 e5 dxe5 fxe5 Ng4
variations.explanation: White pushes four pawns to the fourth rank — looks scary but it's actually committal. Black undermines the center with ...c5 and ...e6. After dxe5, Black gets active piece play and White's overextended center can collapse.

variations.name: Fianchetto Variation
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 g6 Nf3 Bg7 g3 O-O Bg2 d6 O-O Nbd7 Nc3 e5 e4 c6 h3 Qb6 Re1 Re8
variations.explanation: The Fianchetto is a quieter but very dangerous system for White. With g3 and Bg2, White plays for slow positional pressure. Black counters with ...c6 and ...e5, maintaining a solid structure while looking for the ...d5 break.

variations.name: Petrosian System (d5 without e4)
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 g6 Nc3 Bg7 e4 d6 Nf3 O-O Be2 e5 d5 a5 Bg5 h6 Bh4 Na6 Nd2 Qe8 O-O Nh7
variations.explanation: The Petrosian system with d5 and Bg5 tries to restrict Black's kingside play. Black maneuvers with ...a5, ...Na6-c5 and ...Nh7-f6 to reorganize. The position is less forcing but Black's kingside chances remain.

warningLines.name: Ignoring the Queenside
warningLines.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 g6 Nc3 Bg7 e4 d6 Nf3 O-O Be2 e5 O-O Nc6 d5 Ne7 Ne1 Nd7 f3 f5 Be3 f4 Bf2 g5 c5 Ng6 cxd6 cxd6 Nd3 Nf6 Rc1 Rf7 Nb5 g4
warningLines.explanation: If Black focuses only on the kingside and ignores White's queenside play, White can break through first with c5-c6. Always keep an eye on White's queenside advances and time your attack accordingly.

warningLines.name: Premature ...f5 Without Ne7
warningLines.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 g6 Nc3 Bg7 e4 d6 Nf3 O-O Be2 e5 O-O Nc6 d5 Ne7 Ne1 Ng4 f3 Nh6 g4 f5 gxf5 gxf5 Kh1 Kh8 Rg1 Nf7 Be3 fxe4 fxe4 Bh6
warningLines.explanation: Pushing ...f5 without first rerouting the knight via ...Nd7 can leave Black's attack disorganized. Playing ...Ng4 instead allows White to push f3 and g4, clamping down on the kingside. The standard plan is ...Ne7, ...Nd7, then ...f5 with the pieces properly coordinated for the kingside assault.

name: Nimzo-Indian Defence
pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 Nc3 Bb4 Qc2 O-O a3 Bxc3+ Qxc3 d5 Nf3 dxc4 Qxc4 b6 Bg5 Ba6 Qa4 c5 O-O-O Nc6 e4 Bb7 Bd3 Na5 d5 exd5 exd5 Nxd5
overview: The Nimzo-Indian is the crown jewel of Black's 1.d4 defenses. By pinning the knight on c3 with ...Bb4, you immediately fight for control of e4 and create imbalances. The beauty of the Nimzo is its flexibility — you can play solid, aggressive, or positional depending on White's response. Carlsen, Fischer, and virtually every World Champion has played it.
keyIdeas: The Bb4 pin on Nc3 controls e4 indirectly and creates structural imbalances
Black is often happy to give up the dark-squared bishop for White's knight, gaining doubled c-pawns
After Bxc3, White's doubled pawns are a long-term weakness in the endgame
Black's plans include ...c5, ...d5, and ...b6 with Bb7 controlling the long diagonal
traps: After 4.Qc2 O-O 5.a3 Bxc3+ 6.Qxc3 b6, Black prepares ...Bb7 with powerful pressure on the e4 square.
In the Rubinstein (4.e3), Black can play ...c5 and ...d5 getting a great IQP position where the isolated pawn gives dynamic piece play.
warnings: Don't give up the Bb4 without getting structural compensation (doubled pawns or an open file)
Against 4.f3, be ready for a different type of game — White aims for e4 and a big center

trapLines.name: Double Pawns Endgame Trick
trapLines.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 Nc3 Bb4 Qc2 O-O a3 Bxc3+ Qxc3 d5 Nf3 dxc4 Qxc4 b6 Bg5 Ba6 Qa4 h6 Bxf6 Qxf6 e3 Bb7 Be2 Nd7 O-O e5 d5 Nc5 Qa5 Nd3
trapLines.explanation: If White gets careless in the Classical Variation, Black can create threats along the long diagonal and the d-file. The knight maneuvering to d3 via c5 is a powerful idea that disrupts White's coordination.

trapLines.name: e4 Block After Bxc3
trapLines.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 Nc3 Bb4 e3 O-O Bd3 d5 Nf3 c5 O-O dxc4 Bxc4 cxd4 exd4 b6 Bg5 Bb7 Re1 Nbd7 Rc1 Rc8 Qb3 Be7
trapLines.explanation: In the Rubinstein, Black plays ...c5 challenging White's center. After ...dxc4 and ...cxd4, White gets an isolated d-pawn. Black blockades on d5 and targets the weak pawn in the endgame.

variations.name: Classical Variation (4.Qc2)
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 Nc3 Bb4 Qc2 O-O a3 Bxc3+ Qxc3 d5 Nf3 dxc4 Qxc4 b6 Bg5 Ba6
variations.explanation: The Classical with 4.Qc2 prevents the doubled c-pawns. Black responds with ...O-O and ...d5, then develops the light-squared bishop to a6. The position is balanced but requires accurate play from both sides.

variations.name: Rubinstein System (4.e3)
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 Nc3 Bb4 e3 O-O Bd3 d5 Nf3 c5 O-O dxc4 Bxc4 Nbd7 Qe2 b6 Rd1 Bb7
variations.explanation: The Rubinstein is the most popular line. White plays solidly with e3 and develops naturally. Black gets a comfortable game with ...c5 and ...d5. The resulting IQP positions are rich with tactical and strategic ideas.

variations.name: Leningrad Variation (4.Bg5)
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 Nc3 Bb4 Bg5 h6 Bh4 c5 d5 d6 e3 Bxc3+ bxc3 e5 Bd3 Nbd7 Ne2 Qe7 O-O g5 Bg3 Nh5
variations.explanation: White pins the knight with Bg5, trying to control e4. Black plays ...h6 and ...c5, creating tension in the center. The resulting positions are complex with chances for both sides.

variations.name: Kasparov Variation (4.Nf3)
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 Nc3 Bb4 Nf3 c5 g3 cxd4 Nxd4 O-O Bg2 d5 cxd5 Nxd5 Qb3 Qa5 Bd2 Nc6 Nxc6 bxc6
variations.explanation: The Kasparov Variation with 4.Nf3 avoids early commitments. After ...c5 and g3, White fianchettoes and plays for a Catalan-like position. Black responds with ...d5, getting active piece play and the bishop pair.

variations.name: Saemisch Variation (4.a3)
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 Nc3 Bb4 a3 Bxc3+ bxc3 c5 e3 Nc6 Bd3 O-O Ne2 b6 e4 Ne8 O-O Ba6 f4 d6
variations.explanation: White immediately challenges the bishop with 4.a3, accepting doubled c-pawns in exchange for the bishop pair. Black gives up the bishop but gets a solid position with ...c5 and ...b6. The doubled pawns are a long-term target.

warningLines.name: Retreating Bb4 to e7
warningLines.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 Nc3 Bb4 e3 O-O Bd3 d5 Nf3 Be7 O-O b6 cxd5 exd5 Ne5 Bb7 Qf3 c5 dxc5 bxc5 Rd1 Nc6 Nxc6 Bxc6 Nb5 Bxb5 Bxb5
warningLines.explanation: Retreating the bishop to e7 after ...Bb4 wastes two tempi. The whole point of the Nimzo is to pin the knight — if you retreat, White gets a comfortable position with no doubled pawns. Commit to the pin or exchange on c3.

warningLines.name: Playing ...d5 Too Late
warningLines.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 e6 Nc3 Bb4 Qc2 O-O a3 Bxc3+ Qxc3 b6 Bg5 Bb7 e3 d6 Bd3 Nbd7 Ne2 h6 Bh4 e5 d5 a5 Ng3 Nc5 f3 Nxd3+ Qxd3 Nd7
warningLines.explanation: Delaying ...d5 and playing ...d6 instead gives White time to build a strong center. Without the ...d5 break, Black's position becomes cramped and the Bb7 has no scope. Always play ...d5 when you can.

name: Grünfeld Defence
pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 g6 Nc3 d5 cxd5 Nxd5 e4 Nxc3 bxc3 Bg7 Nf3 c5 Be3 Qa5 Qd2 O-O Rc1 cxd4 cxd4 Nc6 Be2 Bg4 O-O Rfd8
overview: The Grünfeld is the ultimate counterattacking defense. You actually encourage White to build a massive pawn center with e4 and d4, then systematically destroy it with ...c5, ...Nc6, and the pressure from the Bg7 on the long diagonal. It's been the primary weapon of Kasparov, Grischuk, and MVL. The positions are incredibly dynamic — White has the center, but Black has the initiative.
keyIdeas: Let White build the center with e4 and d4, then attack it with ...c5 and piece pressure
The Bg7 is the most important piece — it pressures d4 along the long diagonal
After ...Nxc3 bxc3, White has doubled pawns but a strong center
The typical plan is ...c5, ...Nc6, ...Bg4/Qa5 to undermine White's center
traps: In the Exchange Variation, after Be3 Qa5, the c3 pawn is a long-term target. Black pressures it relentlessly.
If White plays Bc4 too early, Black can play ...c5 and ...Nc6 with rapid development and pressure on d4.
warnings: Don't let White consolidate the center with f3 and Be3 — attack immediately with ...c5
The Bg7 must stay on the board — trading it usually favors White

trapLines.name: Exchange Variation c3 Pressure
trapLines.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 g6 Nc3 d5 cxd5 Nxd5 e4 Nxc3 bxc3 Bg7 Nf3 c5 Be3 Qa5 Qd2 O-O Rc1 cxd4 cxd4 Qxd2+ Nxd2 Rd8 d5 e6 Bc4 exd5 Bxd5 Nc6
trapLines.explanation: In the Exchange Variation, Black can grab the a2 pawn with ...Qa5+ and ...Qxa2. The queen is temporarily offside but Black has two extra pawns. White's center looks imposing but Black's piece activity compensates.

trapLines.name: Russian System Nc6 Pressure
trapLines.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 g6 Nc3 d5 Nf3 Bg7 Qb3 dxc4 Qxc4 O-O e4 Nc6 Be2 e5 d5 Nd4 Nxd4 exd4 Nb5 c5 dxc6 bxc6 Nc3 Be6 Qa4 d3 Bf3 Rb8 O-O c5
trapLines.explanation: In the Russian System with Qb3, Black plays ...Nc6 and ...e5, striking at the center. After ...Nd4, Black gets powerful piece play and the d4 pawn becomes a strong passed pawn.

variations.name: Exchange Variation (Main Line)
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 g6 Nc3 d5 cxd5 Nxd5 e4 Nxc3 bxc3 Bg7 Nf3 c5 Be3 Qa5 Qd2 O-O Rc1 Nc6
variations.explanation: The Exchange Variation is the main battleground. White gets a powerful pawn center but doubled c-pawns. Black attacks with ...c5, ...Nc6, and ...Qa5. The positions are incredibly rich and theory goes deep in every line.

variations.name: Russian System (Qb3)
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 g6 Nc3 d5 Nf3 Bg7 Qb3 dxc4 Qxc4 O-O e4 Nc6 Be2 Nd7 Be3 Nb6 Qd3 f5
variations.explanation: White develops the queen to b3 early, hitting d5 and b7. Black plays ...dxc4 and gets rapid development. The fight centers around White's e4 pawn and Black's counterplay with ...Nc6 and ...f5.

variations.name: Fianchetto Variation
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 g6 Nf3 Bg7 g3 d5 Bg2 dxc4 Na3 c3 bxc3 c5 O-O O-O Rb1 Nc6 d5 Na5 Nd2 b6
variations.explanation: White fianchettoes with g3 and Bg2, playing for a slower positional game. Black can hold the c4 pawn temporarily or return it to get quick development. The positions are less forcing but Black has excellent chances.

variations.name: Bc4 Classical
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 g6 Nc3 d5 cxd5 Nxd5 e4 Nxc3 bxc3 Bg7 Bc4 O-O Ne2 c5 O-O Nc6 Be3 Bg4 f3 Bd7
variations.explanation: White develops the bishop to c4 targeting f7. This aggressive approach leads to sharp play where White tries to maintain the center while Black undermines it with ...c5, ...Nc6, and piece pressure.

variations.name: Neo-Grünfeld (g3 without Nc3)
variations.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 g6 g3 d5 Bg2 Bg7 Nf3 O-O O-O dxc4 Na3 c3 bxc3 c5 dxc5 Qa5 c4 Qxc5 Bb2 Nc6
variations.explanation: The Neo-Grünfeld avoids the main theoretical lines. White fianchettoes without Nc3, and the resulting positions are unique to this move order. Black gets active play with ...c5 and the Bg7 is a beast.

warningLines.name: Trading the Dark-Squared Bishop
warningLines.pgn: d4 Nf6 c4 g6 Nc3 d5 cxd5 Nxd5 e4 Nxc3 bxc3 Bg7 Bc4 c5 Ne2 O-O O-O Nc6 Be3 cxd4 cxd4 Na5 Bd3 Be6 d5 Bd7 Nd4 e6 dxe6 fxe6 Qb1 Nc4 Bc1 Qb6
